chemicals.name: The toxic principles in garlic are primarily a complex mixture of sulfur-containing compounds. These substances are exceptionally dangerous to cats

chemicals.concentration_notes: These compounds are incredibly potent in cats. Furthermore, processing methods like drying and granulation significantly concentrate these reactive compounds, making powdered and granulated garlic substantially more dangerous and likely to cause oxidative damage than fresh garlic
chemicals.description: These organosulfur compounds are readily absorbed through the cat's gastrointestinal tract and metabolized into highly reactive oxidants2. They cause severe oxidative damage to the erythrocyte (red blood cell) membranes and hemoglobin3. Because feline red blood cells are highly susceptible to oxidative damage, these oxidants quickly overwhelm the cells' antioxidant defenses, causing the hemoglobin
chemicals.name: Thiosulfates and Disulfides (including Diallyl derivatives)

chemicals.concentration_notes: These are highly active constituents of garlic oil9. Unaged garlic preparations containing these compounds can also cause direct, painful damage to the gastric and ileal mucosa (stomach and intestinal lining), resulting in severe diarrhea
chemicals.description: These are pharmacologically active agents found specifically in garlic. They act as potent cardiac and smooth muscle relaxants, vasodilators, and hypotensive (blood-pressure-lowering) agents8. Ajoene is additionally a potent antithrombotic (blood-thinning) agent8. While they are not the primary cause of red blood cell destruction, their hypotensive and antithrombotic properties severely exacerbate
chemicals.name: Allicin and Ajoene

chemicals.concentration_notes: These compounds are responsible for the characteristic odor and flavor of garlic11. Because they are heavily concentrated throughout the plant, any ingestion of garlic exposes the cat to these toxin-generating precursors
chemicals.description: These are the parent compounds sequestered inside the plant's cells11. When the garlic clove experiences mechanical trauma—such as being chewed by a cat, chopped, or crushed—these precursors instantly convert into the complex mixture of toxic sulfur-containing organic compounds (like allicin and the thiosulfates mentioned above)11. Concentration/Potency notes: These compounds are responsible for t
chemicals.name: Alk(en)yl cysteine sulfoxides (Precursors)

description: Garlic (Allium sativum) is one of the most toxic foods for cats, significantly more dangerous than onion — roughly 3 to 5 times more potent by weight. It contains organosulfur compounds including N-propyl disulfide and allicin derivatives that cats cannot safely metabolise. These compounds oxidise haemoglobin inside red blood cells, forming Heinz bodies and triggering haemolytic anaemia.
emergencyNote: 1. Oxygen Therapy and Blood Transfusions (Life-Saving Stabilization)
name: Garlic
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: N-propyl disulfide, allicin, and allyl disulfides; all forms are dangerous — raw, cooked, dried, or powdered
Mechanism: oxidative damage to haemoglobin creates Heinz bodies; the spleen destroys affected RBCs faster than they can be replaced, causing haemolytic anaemia
Symptoms: lethargy, weakness, pale or yellowish gums, rapid breathing, reduced appetite, reddish-brown urine; onset is often delayed 3–5 days after ingestion
Garlic powder is especially dangerous — concentrated and easily hidden in human food
Never share garlic bread, sauces, broth, or any seasoned food with cats; contact your vet immediately if ingestion is suspected

symptoms.name: Because garlic is a member of the Allium family and contains potent organosulfur compounds, it causes symptoms very similar to onion toxicosis
Vomiting, Diarrhea, Abdominal Pain, and Drooling
symptoms.notes: These are typically the first signs of toxicosis to appear2. Unaged garlic preparations contain specific compounds that can cause direct, painful damage to the gastric and ileal mucosa, resulting in severe abdominal pain and diarrhea45. The toxins can also cause direct irritation to the mouth, leadi
symptoms.onset: Often within 24 hours, though it can be delayed 2 to 4 days

symptoms.name: Lethargy, Weakness, and Depression
symptoms.notes: These symptoms emerge as a secondary response to systemic hypoxia caused by the profound destruction of oxygen-carrying red blood cells (hemolytic anemia)
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days (typically 2 to 4 days)

symptoms.name: Pale Mucous Membranes and Jaundice (Icterus)
symptoms.notes: Pale gums indicate a lack of oxygenated blood (anemia), while jaundice (yellowing of the skin/mucosa) indicates severe red blood cell destruction leading to an excess of bilirubin in the blood (hyperbilirubinemia)
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days

symptoms.name: Tachypnea and Dyspnea (Breathing Difficulties)
symptoms.notes: This is a compensatory response to the decreased blood oxygen transportation capacity and resulting tissue hypoxia
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days

symptoms.name: Tachycardia and Hypotension (Cardiovascular Distress)
symptoms.notes: Tachycardia develops as the heart pumps faster to compensate for impaired oxygen transportation6. Furthermore, garlic contains pharmacologically active agents (allicin and ajoene) that act as potent cardiac and smooth muscle relaxants, vasodilators, and hypotensive agents4. These hypotensive propert

symptoms.name: Hemoglobinuria (Dark Urine)
symptoms.notes: This is a hallmark sign of severe oxidative hemolysis, where damaged red blood cells rupture and release hemoglobin, which is then excreted through the urinary tract
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days

symptoms.name: Cold Sensitivity
symptoms.notes: Affected cats may have difficulty regulating their body temperature due to the systemic effects of the anemia and hypoxia

symptoms.name: Garlic Breath
symptoms.notes: While not dangerous itself, this symptom is specifically associated with recent ingestion and serves as a highly valuable diagnostic clue for veterinarians

treatments.description: of the procedure: Providing the cat with supplemental oxygen and transfusing whole blood to restore the body's depleted oxygen-carrying capacity
treatments.name: 1. Oxygen Therapy and Blood Transfusions (Life-Saving Stabilization)
treatments.notes: This is the most urgent priority for severely affected cats presenting with life-threatening signs such as respiratory distress, systemic hypoxia, or profound hemolytic anemia1.... Blood transfusions may be specifically indicated if the cat's hematocrit drops below 15–20%3. Because there is no speci

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering an emetic drug, typically an α 2 ​ -adrenergic agonist like dexmedetomidine (via intramuscular or intravenous injection), to force the cat to expel the ingested garlic before the toxins are absorbed into the bloodstream
treatments.name: Induction of Emesis (Early Gastrointestinal Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This early decontamination step is highly valuable but only indicated if the ingestion was very recent (typically caught within 1 to 2 hours) and the cat is currently asymptomatic2.... Crucially, 3% hydrogen peroxide must never be used to induce vomiting in cats, as it is strictly contraindicated an

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering oral activated charcoal (typically 1 to 4 g/kg) to bind to and adsorb any residual toxins remaining in the stomach and intestines
treatments.name: Administration of Activated Charcoal (Secondary Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This is typically administered directly after emesis to trap toxins before they can enter the bloodstream1.... However, it should be noted that its actual efficacy in binding to or preventing the production of Allium-derived sulfides is still unknown

treatments.description: of the procedure: Hospitalizing the cat to administer continuous IV fluids to rehydrate the animal, support blood pressure, and maintain proper renal perfusion
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy (Systemic Supportive Care)
treatments.notes: This treatment is indicated if the cat has suffered extensive fluid loss from severe vomiting and diarrhea, or presents with hypotension12. Furthermore, it is a highly recommended protective measure if the cat develops hemoglobinuria (dark urine caused by ruptured red blood cells), as IV diuresis he

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering anti-sickness/anti-nausea medications, stomach protectants, and sometimes antioxidant vitamins (C and E) or N-acetyl-cysteine
treatments.name: Gastrointestinal and Antioxidant Medications (Symptomatic Relief)
treatments.notes: The treatment of garlic poisoning is generally nonspecific and primarily symptomatic11. While antioxidants are sometimes administered in an attempt to combat the oxidative damage to red blood cells, they have shown minimal proven protective effects against Allium toxicity in cats
